label: Tehillim (Psalms) — Part 9 — Psalms 151, 154, 155 — DSS Only
summary_plain: Psalms 151, 154, and 155 are preserved in the Great Psalms Scroll (11QPsa) from Qumran Cave 11. They are NOT in the Masoretic text. Psalm 151 is Dawid's song about being the youngest brother, chosen by YHWH from among the sheep to be anointed over Yisra'EL, and his victory over the Pelishti giant. Psalm 154 calls the congregation to praise YHWH in the assembly of the faithful. Psalm 155 is a personal prayer for deliverance from sin and from the evil impulse. These are scripture preserved in the DSS and in the Orit Ge'ez but excluded from the Masoretic canon.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads Psalms 151, 154, and 155 from 11QPsa (the Great Psalms Scroll, Qumran Cave 11) and from the Orit Ge'ez, where Psalm 151 has been continuously preserved as canonical scripture. The Masoretic Psalter ends at Psalm 150. European Bibles based on the Masoretic therefore exclude these psalms entirely. The Orit Ge'ez never excluded them. The DSS prove the Orit was not adding foreign material — it was preserving what the Masoretic removed. Psalm 151 in 11QPsa is longer than the version preserved in the Septuagint, containing details about Dawid's anointing and his battle with the giant. The Syriac Peshitta also preserves Psalms 151-155 in some manuscripts. The pattern is consistent: the Masoretic is the narrowest canon, not the original canon. Every older and independent witness — DSS, Orit Ge'ez, Syriac — preserves a broader collection.

key_terms.definition: The largest Psalms manuscript from Qumran, found in Cave 11. It preserves Psalms 101-150 in a different order than the Masoretic and includes compositions absent from European Bibles — Psalms 151, 154, 155, and other texts. It is the primary DSS witness for the broader Psalter.

Dawid's autobiographical psalm — he was small among his brothers, YHWH sent his messenger and anointed him, and he went out against the Pelishti. Preserved in 11QPsa (in Hebrew) and in the Orit Ge'ez (in Ge'ez). Excluded from the Masoretic.

Psalm 155 prays for deliverance from the yetzer hara — the inclination toward wrongdoing. This concept is central to the covenant anthropology: the human being has both the impulse toward good and the impulse toward evil. No external savior is needed — YHWH directly strengthens the individual against the evil impulse.

fill_blank.prompt: I was small among my brothers, and the youngest among the sons of my father. He made me ______ of his flocks.
fill_blank.source_quote: I was small among my brothers, and the youngest among the sons of my father. He made me shepherd of his flocks, and ruler over his kids.

fill_blank.prompt: He sent his messenger and took me from my father's sheep, and ______ me with the oil of anointing.
fill_blank.source_quote: He sent his messenger and took me from my father's sheep, and anointed me with the oil of anointing.

fill_blank.prompt: Glorify YHWH with a loud voice — in the ______ of the many proclaim his splendor.
fill_blank.source_quote: Glorify YHWH with a loud voice — in the congregation of the many proclaim his splendor.

faq.question: Why did the Masoretic canon exclude Psalms 151, 154, and 155?
faq.answer: The Masoretic Psalter ends at Psalm 150. Psalms 151, 154, and 155 are preserved in the Dead Sea Scrolls (11QPsa, 1st century BCE) and in the Orit Ge'ez — but the Masoretic canonizers excluded them. This is not an accident. The Masoretic canon was finalized by the Ben Asher family in Tiberias between 850-950 CE — nearly 1,000 years after the DSS were sealed in the caves. The narrowing of the canon is a documented editorial decision. The Orit Ge'ez never excluded Psalm 151 — it has been continuously canonical in the Ethiopian Israelite tradition for millennia. The DSS prove the Orit was preserving older material, not adding later inventions. Every time the Masoretic canon is treated as 'the original,' the excluded material disappears. This is the Great Eraser in its most basic form: defining the boundaries of scripture so that inconvenient texts cease to exist.

faq.question: What does the existence of DSS-only psalms prove about the Masoretic canon?
faq.answer: The existence of Psalms 151, 154, 155, and additional compositions in 11QPsa — all absent from the Masoretic — proves one thing conclusively: the Masoretic canon is NOT the original. It is a later reduction of a broader collection. The DSS are 800-1,000 years older than the Masoretic. The Orit Ge'ez preserves the same broader collection independently. Two ancient witnesses — one from the Judean desert, one from the Ethiopian highlands — agree against the Masoretic. The academic habit of treating the Masoretic as the baseline and everything else as 'additions' or 'apocrypha' reverses the chronology. The broader collection is older. The Masoretic is the reduction. The Great Eraser depends on this reversal — treating the narrowest, latest canon as the standard and everything excluded as secondary.

faq.question: How does Psalm 151 connect to Dawid's anointing narrative?
faq.answer: Psalm 151 in 11QPsa is Dawid speaking in first person: 'I was small among my brothers, and the youngest among the sons of my father. He made me shepherd of his flocks.' YHWH sends his messenger and anoints Dawid. Then Dawid goes out and defeats the Pelishti. This is the 1 Shemu'el 16-17 narrative compressed into a psalm — Dawid's own voice telling his own story. The Masoretic Psalter excludes this. European Bibles therefore contain no first-person psalm from Dawid about his anointing. The 11QPsa also includes a prose composition listing Dawid's total output: 3,600 tehillim (psalms) and 450 songs. The Masoretic preserves 150. The 11QPsa preserves the tradition that most of Dawid's compositions were lost — or excluded.
